enrichment.short_description: A lightweight CLI tool that turns RSS, Atom, and JSON feeds into a single static HTML page.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: TinyFeed is a command-line tool written in Go that fetches a list of RSS, Atom, and JSON feeds and compiles them into one lightweight, static HTML page. Rather than running a server-side reader backed by a database, it produces a plain webpage that can be hosted anywhere -- a cron job, systemd timer, Docker container, or GitHub Actions workflow can regenerate it on a schedule.
It supports custom stylesheets and templates plus a built-in dark/light theme that follows the visitor's system preference, and needs no infrastructure beyond a place to serve a static file.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Self-hosters and developers who want a minimal, database-free way to follow multiple feeds on one page -- inspired by tools like tinystatus and community link-aggregation pages -- without deploying a full-featured feed reader.

features.title: Multi-format feed parsing
features.description: Reads RSS, Atom, and JSON feeds via the gofeed library.

features.title: Static HTML output
features.description: Generates a plain HTML page with no database or backend service required to serve it.

features.title: Custom styling
features.description: Override the default stylesheet or supply custom templates.

features.title: Dark/light theme
features.description: Automatically matches the visitor's OS-level theme preference.

features.title: Scheduled regeneration
features.description: Run via cron, systemd timers, OpenRC, or a GitHub Actions workflow to keep the page current.

features.title: Docker support
features.description: An official container image is available for containerized deployments.

pros: Extremely lightweight -- no database or long-running server process required
Free and open source under the MIT license
Flexible deployment options (cron, Docker, systemd, GitHub Pages/Actions)
Simple to theme and customize with custom templates/CSS
cons: Command-line only -- no built-in web UI for managing feeds
No built-in read-state tracking, search, or per-user accounts since it's a static generator, not a full reader
Requires an external scheduler (cron/systemd/CI) to keep the page up to date

faqs.question: Is TinyFeed free?
faqs.answer: Yes, TinyFeed is open-source software released under the MIT license and free to self-host.

faqs.question: Does TinyFeed require a database?
faqs.answer: No. It reads a feed list and generates a static HTML file, so there's nothing to install beyond the binary or container image.

faqs.question: Can I style the output page?
faqs.answer: Yes, TinyFeed supports custom stylesheets and templates.

faqs.question: How do I keep the page updated?
faqs.answer: Run TinyFeed on a schedule using cron, systemd timers, OpenRC, or a GitHub Actions workflow.

use_cases.title: Personal feed dashboard
use_cases.description: Aggregate blogs and news feeds into one static page for personal browsing.

use_cases.title: Public status/link page
use_cases.description: Publish a curated public feed page, similar to link-aggregator sites, via GitHub Pages.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: TinyFeed is a free, open-source CLI tool that turns RSS, Atom, and JSON feeds into a single lightweight static HTML page.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted tool that generates enriched, filterable RSS feeds from Reddit, Hacker News, Lemmy, and more.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Upvote RSS is an open-source, self-hosted application that builds custom RSS feeds from social aggregation sites -- Reddit, Hacker News, Lemmy, Lobsters, PieFed, Mbin, and GitHub. Instead of consuming a platform's raw firehose, users can filter by minimum score, posts-per-day, or search terms, and the generated feed embeds media, article previews, top comments, and estimated reading time.
It also offers optional AI-generated summaries of linked articles through several LLM providers, including Ollama, OpenAI, Google Gemini, Anthropic, Mistral, and DeepSeek.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: People who want more control over noisy link-aggregator feeds -- filtering out low-engagement posts and reading richer previews in their own feed reader -- and who are comfortable self-hosting a small Docker or Node.js/PHP service.

features.title: Multi-source support
features.description: Pull from Reddit, Hacker News, Lemmy, Lobsters, PieFed, Mbin, and GitHub.

features.title: Score and volume filtering
features.description: Set minimum upvote thresholds or posts-per-day limits to cut down on noise.

features.title: Content enrichment
features.description: Embedded media, parsed article previews, and featured images in each feed item.

features.title: AI article summaries
features.description: Optional integration with Ollama, OpenAI, Google Gemini, Anthropic, Mistral, or DeepSeek to summarize linked articles.

features.title: Top comments
features.description: Pull in top comments per post, with optional filtering of pinned moderator comments.

features.title: NSFW filtering and custom domains
features.description: Blur or hide NSFW content and serve feeds from a custom domain.

features.title: Caching backends
features.description: Filesystem, Redis, or APCu caching for performance.

pros: Free, open-source (MIT license), with no vendor lock-in
Supports many source platforms beyond just Reddit
Optional AI summaries reduce time spent clicking through to articles
Quick to self-host via a single Docker command
cons: Requires self-hosting (Docker or a PHP 8.2+ environment) -- no managed hosted tier confirmed
AI summary feature requires bringing your own LLM provider/API key
No native mobile app -- consumed through a regular feed reader

faqs.question: What platforms can Upvote RSS pull from?
faqs.answer: Reddit, Hacker News, Lemmy, Lobsters, PieFed, Mbin, and GitHub.

faqs.question: Is Upvote RSS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's open source under the MIT license and free to self-host.

faqs.question: Do I need an LLM API key to use it?
faqs.answer: Only if you enable the optional AI article summary feature; core feed generation and filtering work without one.

faqs.question: How do I deploy it?
faqs.answer: The recommended method is Docker (ghcr.io/johnwarne/upvote-rss); manual installation requires PHP 8.2 or newer.

use_cases.title: Curated tech news feed
use_cases.description: Filter Hacker News, Lemmy, or Lobsters posts by score to cut noise in a feed reader.

use_cases.title: Community monitoring
use_cases.description: Track high-engagement posts across specific subreddits or GitHub repositories.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Upvote RSS is a free, open-source self-hosted tool that turns Reddit, Hacker News, Lemmy, and other feeds into filtered, enriched RSS with optional AI summaries.

enrichment.short_description: A lightweight, open-source personal cloud alternative to Nextcloud/ownCloud, built with TypeScript and Deno.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: bewCloud is an open-source personal cloud platform positioned as a simpler, lighter alternative to Nextcloud and ownCloud. Built entirely in TypeScript on Deno, it bundles a set of everyday personal apps -- file storage, notes, photos, calendar, contacts, a news/feed reader, and an expense tracker called Envelopes -- behind a single dashboard.

It's created and maintained by developer Bruno Bernardino, with support from the NLnet Foundation and GitHub Sponsors. The self-hosted software is free; a managed VPS hosting option is also offered for people who'd rather not run their own server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Individuals and small households who want a self-hosted alternative to Nextcloud/ownCloud with a lighter resource footprint and a smaller, more focused app set, and who are comfortable running a Deno-based server -- or prefer to pay for a managed VPS instead.

features.title: File storage
features.description: Web-based file browser and storage for personal files.

features.title: Notes
features.description: A built-in notes app for quick documents and text.

features.title: Photos
features.description: Organize and view photo collections.

features.title: Calendar
features.description: Personal calendar and scheduling.

features.title: Contacts
features.description: Manage personal contacts.

features.title: News
features.description: A feed reader for following RSS/news content.

features.title: Envelopes
features.description: A simple budgeting and expense-tracking tool.

features.title: Dashboard
features.description: A central hub tying all the bundled apps together.

pros: Lighter resource footprint than Nextcloud/ownCloud, aimed at simpler self-hosting
Fully open source and free to self-host
Covers common personal-cloud needs -- files, notes, photos, calendar, contacts, expenses -- in one app
Optional managed VPS hosting available for people who don't want to self-host
cons: Smaller plugin/app ecosystem than Nextcloud, which has a much larger third-party app marketplace
Newer, smaller project with a single primary maintainer
Managed hosting pricing isn't published on the marketing site

faqs.question: Is bewCloud free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the self-hosted version is free and open source; a paid managed VPS hosting option is also offered for those who don't want to self-host.

faqs.question: What apps does bewCloud include?
faqs.answer: Files, Notes, Photos, Calendar, Contacts, News, and an expense tracker called Envelopes.

faqs.question: What's it built with?
faqs.answer: TypeScript running on the Deno runtime.

faqs.question: Is it a drop-in Nextcloud replacement?
faqs.answer: It covers similar core personal-cloud use cases but has a much smaller feature and app set than Nextcloud.

use_cases.title: Personal cloud for a household
use_cases.description: Self-host files, photos, calendar, and contacts on your own server.

use_cases.title: Simple budgeting alongside file storage
use_cases.description: Use Envelopes for expense tracking next to your files and notes.
use_cases.audience: Self-hosters wanting an all-in-one personal dashboard

enrichment.seo_meta_description: bewCloud is a free, open-source personal cloud app (files, notes, photos, calendar, contacts, expenses) built as a lighter alternative to Nextcloud.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted cloud storage system supporting local disk, S3, OneDrive, and other backends behind one web UI.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Cloudreve is an open-source, self-hosted cloud storage and file-sharing system written in Go with a React front end. It lets you run your own 'cloud drive' backed by local disk storage or a range of third-party object storage providers -- including S3-compatible APIs, OneDrive, Aliyun OSS, Tencent COS, Huawei OBS, and Qiniu Kodo -- through one consistent web interface.

It supports direct client-to-storage transfers, WebDAV access, background downloading via Aria2/qBittorrent, and in-browser previews and editing for documents, images, Markdown, and Office files. The project is licensed under GPL v3.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Self-hosters and small teams who want a Google-Drive-like web interface on top of their own storage -- local disk or cloud object storage -- with multi-user support, sharing links, and WebDAV compatibility.

features.title: Multiple storage backends
features.description: Local disk, S3-compatible storage, OneDrive, Aliyun OSS, Tencent COS, Huawei OBS, and Qiniu Kodo.

features.title: WebDAV support
features.description: Access any configured storage backend via WebDAV from standard sync clients.

features.title: Direct client-to-storage transfer
features.description: Uploads and downloads can bypass the app server to reduce load.

features.title: Background downloading
features.description: Integrates with Aria2 or qBittorrent for remote/background downloads.

features.title: Online preview and editing
features.description: View and edit documents, images, Markdown, and Office files directly in the browser.

features.title: Compression and batch operations
features.description: Compress, extract, and batch-download files and archives.

features.title: Multi-user accounts
features.description: User groups and permissions for shared or team deployments.

features.title: Shareable links
features.description: Generate share links with expiration controls.

pros: Supports a wide range of storage backends, not just local disk
Actively developed, GPL v3 open-source license
WebDAV compatibility makes it usable from standard file-sync clients
Docker-based deployment is straightforward
cons: Admin/setup requires familiarity with Docker and reverse proxy configuration
Primary documentation and community skew heavily Chinese-language, though English docs exist
No official native mobile apps -- accessed via web or WebDAV instead

faqs.question: Is Cloudreve free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is open source under the GPL v3 license.

faqs.question: What storage backends does it support?
faqs.answer: Local disk plus S3-compatible storage, OneDrive, Aliyun OSS, Tencent COS, Huawei OBS, and Qiniu Kodo.

faqs.question: Can I access Cloudreve via WebDAV?
faqs.answer: Yes, WebDAV is supported across all configured storage backends.

faqs.question: How is it deployed?
faqs.answer: Typically via Docker, with binaries also available for direct installation.

use_cases.title: Personal cloud drive on your own object storage
use_cases.description: Front an S3-compatible bucket with a Drive-like web UI.

use_cases.title: Small team file sharing
use_cases.description: Multi-user accounts and shareable links for a small organization.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cloudreve is a free, open-source, self-hosted cloud storage system supporting local disk, S3, OneDrive, and other backends via one web interface.

enrichment.short_description: A free, open-source Git extension for tracking, syncing, and archiving large files without storing content in Git.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: git-annex extends Git to manage large files -- videos, datasets, disk images -- without storing their content directly in the Git repository. Git tracks only symlinks/pointers and checksums, while the actual file content is distributed across multiple repositories, drives, and remote storage services, including encrypted cloud 'special remotes'.

This lets users sync, back up, and archive large collections across many devices while Git tracks which copies exist where. It's Free Software written in Haskell, with a documentation wiki hosted on Branchable.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Researchers, archivists, photographers, and technical users managing large file collections who want Git-like version tracking and distributed syncing without bloating a Git repository with binary content.

features.title: Large-file tracking
features.description: Track large files in Git without storing their content in the repository itself.

features.title: Distributed sync
features.description: Sync file content across multiple repositories, drives, and remote services.

features.title: Special remotes
features.description: Store content in many backends, including encrypted cloud storage remotes.

features.title: Location tracking
features.description: Know which of your repositories currently hold a copy of a given file.

features.title: Offline archival
features.description: Archive and verify large collections with checksum verification, even offline.

features.title: GUI assistant
features.description: An optional graphical assistant for users who prefer not to use the command line.

features.title: Git-native workflow
features.description: Built directly on top of standard Git commands and repositories.

pros: Free and open source, actively maintained
Works across Linux, macOS, Windows, and Android
Strong fit for large-file archival where full copies don't need to live everywhere
Encryption support for untrusted cloud remotes
cons: Steeper learning curve than plain Git or typical file-sync tools
Command-line-first design; the GUI assistant is more limited
Requires a working understanding of Git concepts to use effectively

faqs.question: Is git-annex free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's Free Software written in Haskell.

faqs.question: Does it store my files in Git?
faqs.answer: No -- Git only tracks pointers and checksums; the actual file content lives in the configured remotes.

faqs.question: What platforms does it support?
faqs.answer: Linux, macOS, Windows, and Android.

faqs.question: Can I encrypt files stored in cloud remotes?
faqs.answer: Yes, git-annex supports encryption for its special remotes.

use_cases.title: Research data archival
use_cases.description: Version and distribute large datasets across lab machines without bloating Git history.

use_cases.title: Personal media archive
use_cases.description: Track and sync large photo/video collections across multiple drives and cloud remotes.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: git-annex is a free, open-source Git extension for tracking, syncing, and archiving large files across devices without storing content in Git.

enrichment.short_description: An open-source, self-hosted, minimalist JSON document storage service with built-in synchronization and sharing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Kinto is a lightweight, self-hosted backend service for storing and synchronizing JSON documents over HTTP. Data is organized into buckets, collections, and records, with fine-grained, inheritable permissions controlling who can read or write each level.

It exposes a full REST API and is designed to be a simple, generic storage layer that client applications can sync against, rather than a full application in itself. The project continues to receive regular releases.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Developers building applications that need a simple, self-hosted, syncable JSON data store with sharing and permissions, without standing up a full custom backend from scratch.

features.title: Generic JSON storage
features.description: Store JSON documents organized into buckets, collections, and records.

features.title: Built-in synchronization
features.description: Sync support designed for offline-capable client applications.

features.title: Inheritable permissions
features.description: Fine-grained, inheritable permissions for sharing data between users.

features.title: Full REST API
features.description: An HTTP API covers all storage and permission operations.

features.title: Admin interface and CLI
features.description: Tooling for managing data outside of a custom client application.

features.title: Client libraries
features.description: Libraries available for building sync-aware applications against Kinto.

pros: Free, open-source, and actively maintained with regular releases
Simple, generic API that's easy to build custom clients against
Built-in permissions model for sharing data between users
Lightweight compared to running a full custom backend
cons: Requires developer effort to build a usable client application on top of it -- it's infrastructure, not an end-user app
Smaller community/ecosystem than larger backend-as-a-service platforms
Documentation assumes familiarity with REST APIs and self-hosting

faqs.question: Is Kinto free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's open-source software, free to self-host.

faqs.question: What kind of data does Kinto store?
faqs.answer: Generic JSON documents, organized into buckets, collections, and records.

faqs.question: Does Kinto support syncing offline clients?
faqs.answer: Yes, synchronization is a core built-in capability.

faqs.question: Is Kinto still maintained?
faqs.answer: Yes, the project continues to publish regular releases.

use_cases.title: Backend for a sync-capable app
use_cases.description: Use Kinto's HTTP API as the storage and sync layer for a custom web or mobile client.

use_cases.title: Shared configuration/data store
use_cases.description: Store and share structured JSON data between services with permission controls.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Kinto is a free, open-source, self-hosted JSON document storage service with built-in synchronization, sharing, and a full REST API.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted file sharing and collaboration platform built by former ownCloud Infinite Scale engineers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenCloud is an open-source file sharing and collaboration platform built on a fork of ownCloud Infinite Scale (OCIS). It was created by OpenCloud GmbH, founded in September 2024 by the Berlin-based Heinlein Group, after roughly 15 engineers who originally built the Go-based ownCloud Infinite Scale rewrite left to continue the project independently.

OpenCloud is published under the Apache 2.0 and AGPL-3.0 licenses and can be self-hosted on-premise or accessed through SaaS partners, with a commercial support/subscription option for organizations that need it.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Businesses, public-sector organizations, educational institutions, and research/science organizations that want a self-hosted, GDPR-compliant file-sharing platform with digital-sovereignty guarantees, backed by a commercial support option.

features.title: Cloud-native file sharing
features.description: File storage and sharing with real-time collaboration, built on the OCIS architecture.

features.title: Integrated office suite
features.description: Edit documents directly in the browser without a separate office application.

features.title: Desktop sync client
features.description: Native sync clients for Windows, macOS, and Linux.

features.title: iOS and Android apps
features.description: Native mobile apps with offline file access and automatic photo backup.

features.title: End-to-end encryption and 2FA
features.description: Encryption and two-factor authentication for account and data security.

features.title: Secure versioning
features.description: File versioning designed to aid recovery from ransomware incidents.

features.title: GDPR-focused, on-premise deployment
features.description: Deploy on your own infrastructure to keep data under EU data-sovereignty requirements.

pros: Backed by an experienced team -- the original ownCloud Infinite Scale engineers
Open source with commercial support available for enterprises
Full platform coverage: web, desktop (Windows/macOS/Linux), iOS, and Android apps
Strong focus on data sovereignty and GDPR compliance for EU organizations
cons: A young project, founded in 2024 and launched in 2025, so its track record is still being established
Detailed pricing for enterprise/support tiers isn't published openly
Migrating from ownCloud or other platforms requires planning

faqs.question: Is OpenCloud free?
faqs.answer: The core platform is open source (Apache 2.0/AGPL-3.0) and free to self-host; paid enterprise support and SaaS hosting through partners are also offered.

faqs.question: How is OpenCloud related to ownCloud?
faqs.answer: It's built on a fork of ownCloud Infinite Scale, created by engineers who originally developed that product.

faqs.question: Who develops OpenCloud?
faqs.answer: OpenCloud GmbH, founded by the Heinlein Group in Berlin in September 2024.

faqs.question: Does OpenCloud have mobile apps?
faqs.answer: Yes, native iOS and Android apps are available alongside the web and desktop clients.

use_cases.title: Enterprise file sharing with data sovereignty
use_cases.description: Self-host OpenCloud on-premise to keep company data under EU/GDPR control.

use_cases.title: Research collaboration
use_cases.description: Share and collaboratively edit files across research teams.

features: Web, desktop, iOS, and Android clients

enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenCloud is an open-source, self-hosted file sharing and collaboration platform built by the original ownCloud Infinite Scale team, focused on GDPR-friendly hosting.

enrichment.short_description: The sftp-server component of OpenSSH, providing secure file transfer over SSH on virtually every platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenSSH is a free, open-source implementation of the SSH protocol suite developed by the OpenBSD project since 1999. Among its components is sftp-server, the server-side process that implements the SFTP (SSH File Transfer Protocol) subsystem, allowing clients to securely browse, upload, download, and manage files over an encrypted SSH connection.
It runs as a subsystem of sshd and requires no separate FTP daemon or unencrypted file-transfer protocol -- file transfer rides on the same encrypted channel used for SSH logins.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: System administrators and self-hosters who need secure, encrypted file transfer to a Linux, BSD, macOS, or Windows server without deploying a separate FTP/FTPS service.

features.title: Encrypted file transfer
features.description: Secure file transfer over an already-encrypted SSH connection.

features.title: Runs as an sshd subsystem
features.description: No separate daemon or additional network port is required.

features.title: Chroot support
features.description: Restrict specific users to a directory using ChrootDirectory.

features.title: Fine-grained access control
features.description: Control permissions and forced commands per user via sshd_config.

features.title: Cross-platform availability
features.description: Native on OpenBSD, with portable builds for Linux, macOS, and Windows (Win32-OpenSSH).

features.title: Logging and auditing
features.description: File transfer activity is logged through sshd.

pros: Free and open source under a permissive BSD-style license
Ships by default or is readily available on nearly every major OS, including Windows 10 and later
No separate protocol or port to open -- uses the same encrypted channel as SSH
Mature, heavily audited codebase maintained by the OpenBSD project
cons: Configuration is done via text files (sshd_config), with no built-in web UI
Chroot/jail setup for restricted users can be fiddly to configure correctly
Feature set is deliberately minimal compared to dedicated managed-file-transfer products

faqs.question: Is the OpenSSH SFTP server free?
faqs.answer: Yes, OpenSSH is free, open-source software.

faqs.question: Do I need a separate FTP server to use SFTP?
faqs.answer: No, sftp-server runs as a subsystem of sshd, the same daemon that handles SSH logins.

faqs.question: What platforms does it run on?
faqs.answer: OpenBSD natively, plus portable builds for Linux, macOS, and Windows (Win32-OpenSSH).

faqs.question: Can I restrict a user to only SFTP with no shell access?
faqs.answer: Yes, via sshd_config options such as ForceCommand and ChrootDirectory.

use_cases.title: Secure file uploads for a web server
use_cases.description: Allow deploy or content users encrypted file transfer without shell access.

use_cases.title: Server-to-server backups
use_cases.description: Transfer backup archives securely between machines.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenSSH's sftp-server provides free, open-source, encrypted SFTP file transfer as a built-in subsystem of sshd on Linux, macOS, BSD, and Windows.

enrichment.short_description: An open-source, self-hostable 'internet OS' offering cloud storage, built-in apps, and an AI API in one platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Puter is an open-source, self-hostable platform described by its creators as an advanced internet computer -- combining cloud file storage, a desktop-like web interface, built-in apps (Notepad, Voice Recorder, Camera, Music Player, Spreadsheet, and more), and a developer backend called Puter.js that offers cloud storage, a key-value database, serverless workers, and an AI Gateway with access to over 500 AI models.
It's maintained by Puter Technologies Inc. under the AGPL-3.0 license and can be used at puter.com or self-hosted via a one-line install script or by running the open-source code directly.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Developers who want a free backend -- storage, database, AI -- for building web apps without managing their own infrastructure, and self-hosters who want a browser-based personal desktop for files and apps.

features.title: Web-based desktop environment
features.description: Built-in apps such as Notepad, Voice Recorder, Camera, Music Player, and Spreadsheet.

features.title: Puter.js developer library
features.description: Add cloud storage, a database, and serverless workers to any web app without running your own backend.

features.title: AI Gateway
features.description: A single API for chat, text-to-image, image-to-text, text-to-speech, and text-to-video across 500+ models.

features.title: App Store
features.description: Developers can publish apps built on Puter and reach its user base.

features.title: Self-hosting options
features.description: One-line install scripts for Linux/macOS/Windows, or run from the open-source repository.

features.title: User-pays resource model
features.description: End users cover their own storage and AI usage, so developers aren't billed for infrastructure.

pros: Open source (AGPL-3.0) with a genuine self-hosting path, not just a hosted SaaS
Free tier available on the hosted puter.com service
AI Gateway gives developers access to many models through one simple API
Active project with a large GitHub community
cons: The user-pays AI/storage model means costs can vary per end user rather than being fixed
As a broad, multi-purpose platform, some built-in apps are more polished than others
Self-hosting the full platform requires running a Node.js-based server stack

faqs.question: Is Puter free?
faqs.answer: The hosted version at puter.com has a free tier, and the self-hosted, open-source version is free to run under the AGPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Puter?
faqs.answer: Yes, via a one-line install script for Linux/macOS/Windows or by cloning and running the open-source repository.

faqs.question: What is Puter.js?
faqs.answer: A JavaScript library that lets developers add cloud storage, a database, serverless workers, and AI capabilities to any web app without managing backend infrastructure.

faqs.question: How does Puter charge for AI usage?
faqs.answer: Through a user-pays model -- each authenticated user covers their own AI and storage usage from their Puter account allowance.

use_cases.title: Personal web-based desktop
use_cases.description: Self-host Puter as a private, browser-accessible file and app environment.

use_cases.title: Backend-free app development
use_cases.description: Use Puter.js for storage, database, and AI without standing up your own servers.

features: Cloud storage and apps on puter.com
Access to built-in apps and the App Store
Full control over storage and AI provider configuration

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Puter is an open-source, self-hostable internet OS offering cloud storage, built-in apps, and an AI API (Puter.js) with a free hosted tier.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source implementation of the SMB and Active Directory protocols for file and print sharing on Linux/Unix.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Samba is a free, open-source suite that implements the SMB/CIFS networking protocol and Active Directory services, letting Linux and Unix systems share files and printers with Windows clients and integrate into Windows domains. Originally created by Andrew Tridgell in 1992, it can act as a file/print server, a domain controller, or a member server, and supports authentication via LDAP and Kerberos.

Samba is distributed under the GNU General Public License and developed by a worldwide community. It's a member project of the Software Freedom Conservancy and receives funding support from the Sovereign Tech Fund.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: System administrators who need Linux/Unix servers to share files and printers with Windows machines, or who need to run or join an Active Directory domain without a Windows Server license.

features.title: SMB/CIFS file and print sharing
features.description: Share files and printers between Linux/Unix and Windows clients.

features.title: Active Directory roles
features.description: Operate as an AD Domain Controller or as a domain member server.

features.title: LDAP and Kerberos authentication
features.description: Integrate with standard directory and authentication protocols.

features.title: Cluster filesystem support
features.description: High-performance distributed filesystem support for large deployments.

features.title: Access control
features.description: Fine-grained share-level and file-level permissions.

features.title: Broad interoperability
features.description: Used as the interoperability layer inside cloud PaaS and software-defined storage products.

pros: Free and open source under the GPL, with no licensing fees
Mature, battle-tested software with decades of production use
Full Active Directory domain controller capability, not just file sharing
Backed by an active community and dedicated funding sources, including the Software Freedom Conservancy and Sovereign Tech Fund
cons: Configuration (smb.conf) is text-based with a learning curve for AD/domain setups
Runs on Linux/Unix -- it isn't a Windows-native product, and administration differs from native AD tooling
Large deployments involving clustering or complex AD topologies require significant Samba-specific expertise

faqs.question: Is Samba free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free software distributed under the GNU General Public License.

faqs.question: Can Samba act as an Active Directory domain controller?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports both AD Domain Controller and member server roles.

faqs.question: What platforms does Samba run on?
faqs.answer: Linux and other Unix-like systems, providing interoperability with SMB protocol clients such as Windows.

faqs.question: Who maintains Samba?
faqs.answer: An active worldwide open-source community; the project is part of the Software Freedom Conservancy and receives support from the Sovereign Tech Fund.

use_cases.title: Cross-platform file server
use_cases.description: Share files and printers between Linux servers and Windows desktops.

use_cases.title: Active Directory replacement
use_cases.description: Run Samba as a domain controller without a Windows Server license.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Samba is a free, open-source implementation of the SMB and Active Directory protocols, enabling Linux/Unix servers to share files with Windows clients.
